purpose: Structured tennis coaching knowledge, keyed to the exact metric ids, strokes, phases and deviation directions the RallyCoach engine produces (lib/core/engine/, python_lab/engine_math.py). For every metric the engine can measure from a 2D MoveNet pose, this file gives the biomechanical rationale, professional benchmarks (with literature sources), the faults that show up when the value runs low or high, their root causes, the on-court fix, a one-line feel cue, and the drill ids that target them. A deviation dict {id, phase, direction} maps directly into metrics[id].faults[direction].

angle_conventions.elbow_angle: Interior angle shoulder-elbow-wrist; 180 = straight arm. Sports-science papers usually report ELBOW FLEXION where 0 = straight, so interior = 180 - flexion (e.g. 30 deg flexion at serve impact = 150 deg interior).
angle_conventions.knee_flexion: Interior angle hip-knee-ankle; 180 = straight leg. Papers report KNEE FLEXION where 0 = straight, so interior = 180 - flexion (e.g. 64.5 deg flexion at the serve trophy = ~115 deg interior).
angle_conventions.trunk_tilt: Deviation of the shoulder-mid -> hip-mid line from vertical, in degrees (0 = upright).
angle_conventions.shoulder_turn: Rotation of the shoulder line away from its address angle, in degrees (90 = chest to the side fence).
angle_conventions.hip_shoulder_sep: Shoulder turn minus hip turn (the 'X-factor'), in degrees.
angle_conventions.contact_in_front: Dominant-wrist x at contact in torso units; positive = in front of the body line.
angle_conventions.contact_height: Dominant-wrist height above hip-mid at contact, in torso units.
angle_conventions.wrist_finish_height: Dominant-wrist height above hip-mid at the end of the follow-through, in torso units.
angle_conventions.toss_height: Tossing-wrist height above hip-mid at the toss release peak, in torso units.
angle_conventions.wrist_divergence: Tossing-wrist minus racket-wrist height at the toss peak, in torso units.

sensing_notes.measurable: MoveNet returns 17 body keypoints. RallyCoach can therefore measure JOINT angles and POSITIONS: arm extension (elbow), leg bend (knee), trunk lean, shoulder/hip rotation and their separation, wrist position at contact and finish, swing/contact height, toss height, preparation timing, and footwork (split step, stance width, recovery). A second tracker models the RACQUET as a rigid extension of the forearm (handle->throat->tip), adding the racquet angle and reach through the swing (metrics racquet_angle, racquet_height, racquet_drop).
sensing_notes.racquet_tracking: The racquet has no MoveNet keypoints, so it is tracked as a rigid forearm extension: the frame leaves the hand at the wrist and continues ~1.35 torso lengths along the elbow->wrist line, giving a handle, throat and frame tip in the same units as the body. This is drawn live over the player and feeds racquet_angle (shaft angle from vertical at contact) and racquet_height (tip reach). It reads the racquet-ARM line and gross orientation - NOT the open/closed face twist or grip. A `racquet_confidence` (0-1) reports how sure the tracker is a racquet was actually swung (racquet-head sweep + arm extension); when it is low the coach hedges instead of confidently coaching a non-shot. Bundling an optical racquet detector later upgrades every racquet metric in place (measured handle/tip keypoints replace the estimate) and makes racquet_confidence an authoritative presence gate.
sensing_notes.not_measurable: Even with the racquet tracker, true WRIST ANGLE (flexion/extension, radial/ulnar deviation), GRIP type, racket-FACE angle (open/closed twist), ball spin, ball speed and net clearance CANNOT be sensed from a 2D body pose. The racquet estimate gives the racquet-arm LINE and orientation, not the face twist. Knowledge about grip and wrist/face action is delivered as spoken/text coaching only, never as a measured score. The closest measurable proxies for 'wrist' are wrist POSITION metrics: contact_in_front, contact_height and wrist_finish_height.
sensing_notes.view_gating: Rotation-separation (hip_shoulder_sep) and contact-depth (contact_in_front) are only reliable from certain camera angles, so the engine view-gates them; racquet_angle is gated the same way for groundstrokes and volleys. From a flat side view forehand/backhand discrimination is geometrically ambiguous; the coach falls back to footwork-only cues.

shoulder_turn.label: Shoulder turn (unit / body turn)
shoulder_turn.biomechanics: The shoulder turn is the first big link in the kinetic chain after the legs. Coiling the trunk away from the net stretches the core and stores elastic energy that the body unwinds into the ball; without it players 'arm' the shot and lose both pace and consistency. Turning the shoulders also sets the racket back early, which is why a full turn and early preparation travel together.

pro_benchmarks.context: groundstroke preparation, chest roughly to the side fence
pro_benchmarks.value: ~90 deg of shoulder rotation

pro_benchmarks.context: shoulder internal rotation contributes a large share of forehand racket speed
pro_benchmarks.value: ~35% of racket speed in advanced players

low.name: Under-rotated / open shoulders
low.looks_like: Chest still facing the net at the backswing; the racket arm swings without a coiled body behind it.
causes: Late reaction / watching the ball instead of turning
Reaching with the arm instead of turning the trunk
No early unit turn off the split step
low.fix: Start the turn the instant the ball leaves the opponent's strings, not when it bounces. Turn the shoulders as one unit until your chin is near your lead shoulder.
low.feel: Show your back (or at least your side) to the net before the bounce.

high.name: Over-rotated shoulders
high.looks_like: Shoulders wound so far past square that the player can't recover the racket to contact in time.
causes: Losing sight of the ball during the coil
high.fix: Keep the turn full but compact - coil to load, not to wind up a baseball swing. Same intent, smaller arc.
high.feel: Turn to load, then let it fire; don't keep winding.

knee_flexion.label: Knee bend (leg drive base)
knee_flexion.biomechanics: Power in tennis is built from the ground up. Bent knees lower the centre of mass, load the legs like springs and let the player drive UP and through contact, feeding the kinetic chain. Stiff legs force the arm to supply all the power and make contact height hostage to the bounce.

pro_benchmarks.context: front-knee flexion at the serve trophy position (max load)
pro_benchmarks.value: 64.5 +/- 9.7 deg flexion (~115 deg interior hip-knee-ankle)

pro_benchmarks.context: deeper knee bend on the serve increases vertical drive and serve speed
pro_benchmarks.value: intermediate players gained measurable serve speed with greater knee flexion

low.name: Too upright / stiff legs (interior angle too large)
low.looks_like: Tall, straight-legged stance; little to no leg drive into the shot.
causes: Fatigue / laziness late in a session
Standing tall to 'reach' the ball instead of moving the feet
low.fix: Sit into an athletic squat as you prepare, then drive up through contact. Load the legs, not the arm.
low.feel: Brush a low chair with your free hand between shots; stay down through the hit.

high.name: Over-squatting (interior angle too small)
high.looks_like: Sinking so low the player is slow to rise and loses spring and balance.
high.fix: Bend enough to load and spring, not to sit. Stay light and springy.
high.feel: Athletic and bouncy, not parked in a deep squat.

trunk_tilt.label: Trunk lean (balance / posture)
trunk_tilt.biomechanics: A controlled, slightly forward trunk tilt lets the player attack the ball and stack the kinetic chain; an excessive or sideways lean pulls the head and eyes off the ball, leaks balance and makes the swing path inconsistent. Staying tall and balanced through contact keeps the strike repeatable.

pro_benchmarks.context: groundstroke address/contact - slight forward tilt into the court
pro_benchmarks.value: roughly 0-20 deg from vertical

low.name: Too upright / leaning back
low.looks_like: Standing bolt upright or falling backwards away from the ball.
causes: Pulling off the shot / opening up too early
Late contact forcing a backward lean
low.fix: Keep the chin level and lean lightly into the court; finish with the chest toward the target.
low.feel: Nose over the front foot, not behind the hips.

high.name: Over-leaning / falling away
high.looks_like: Excessive sideways or forward collapse, losing balance through the finish.
high.fix: Stay balanced - control the lean so you can hold the finish for a beat.
high.feel: If you can't freeze your finish, you weren't balanced.

elbow_angle.label: Arm extension (elbow angle)
elbow_angle.biomechanics: The elbow angle sets your contact distance and leverage. A cramped (small) elbow angle drags contact in toward the body and shortens the lever; an over-extended (locked) arm costs control and loads the joint. On the serve the arm is deliberately bent in the trophy and racket-drop, then snaps toward extension at impact as the kinetic chain releases.

pro_benchmarks.value: ~30.1 +/- 15.9 deg flexion (~150 deg interior; near but not fully locked)

pro_benchmarks.context: internal shoulder rotation, not elbow extension, supplies most racket speed at serve impact
pro_benchmarks.value: elbow stays slightly flexed at contact

pro_benchmarks.context: straight-arm vs flexed-arm forehand shoulder angle (both are viable pro patterns)
pro_benchmarks.value: straight ~79 deg, flexed ~53 deg at the shoulder

low.name: Arm too bent at contact (interior angle too small)
low.looks_like: Elbow tucked in, contact crowded into the body, no extension through the ball.
causes: Pulling the arm in instead of extending through
low.fix: Extend through contact and meet the ball a comfortable arm's length out in front.
low.feel: Reach through the ball toward the target, then let it wrap.

high.name: Arm locked / over-extended
high.looks_like: Rigid straight arm, no give in the elbow, reaching for a ball that is too far away.
causes: Reaching for a wide ball instead of moving the feet
high.fix: Keep a touch of give in the elbow and move your feet so the ball arrives in your zone.
high.feel: Soft, springy elbow - don't snap it straight.

hip_shoulder_sep.label: Hip-shoulder separation (X-factor)
hip_shoulder_sep.biomechanics: The X-factor is the difference between how far the shoulders turn and how far the hips turn. Stretching the shoulders past the hips on the takeback pre-tensions the core; when the hips fire first on the way back to the ball, that stored stretch releases as 'effortless' racket speed. Too little separation = pushing the ball; too much, uncoordinated, breaks timing.

pro_benchmarks.value: ~30 deg
~20 deg

low.name: Body turns as one block (no coil)
low.looks_like: Hips and shoulders rotate together; no stretch across the core.
low.fix: Coil the shoulders past the hips on the takeback, then let the hips lead back to the ball.
low.feel: Shoulders wind, hips wait, then hips fire first.

high.name: Over-separation / timing break
high.looks_like: Shoulders wound far past the hips but the unwind is mistimed, so the energy leaks.
causes: Hips and shoulders out of sequence
high.fix: Ease the coil and match the unwind - separation only helps if the sequence is clean.
high.feel: Coil to a stretch you can release on time, not a maximum.

contact_in_front.label: Contact point in front
contact_in_front.biomechanics: Meeting the ball in front of the body lets the player see the ball, transfer body weight forward into the shot, and control direction. Late contact (level with or behind the hip) means the ball is playing the player: weight goes backward, the arm decelerates, and errors spray.

pro_benchmarks.context: forehand contact ahead of the body (pros)
pro_benchmarks.value: ~30-40 cm in front

pro_benchmarks.context: two-handed backhand contact in front (closer, more reaction time)

low.name: Late contact (too close / behind)
low.looks_like: Ball caught level with or behind the hip; weight falling backward.
low.fix: Meet the ball earlier - step and catch it at the peak of the front foot, out in front.
low.feel: Strike before the ball gets deep; contact in your field of vision.

high.name: Reaching too far in front
high.looks_like: Stretching forward for a contact point too far ahead, losing balance and control.
high.fix: Let the ball travel a touch - still in front, but where you can stay balanced.
high.feel: Patient: it will arrive in your strike zone.

contact_height.label: Contact / strike height
contact_height.biomechanics: Groundstrokes are grooved around a comfortable strike zone (roughly waist height); serves and overheads want a high, fully stretched contact. Hitting outside the grooved zone changes the geometry of the whole swing. On the serve a higher impact lets internal-shoulder-rotation strength convert into serve speed.

pro_benchmarks.value: contact at ~100-110 deg of arm abduction, near full stretch

pro_benchmarks.context: serve impact height is the biomechanical link from shoulder strength to serve speed
pro_benchmarks.value: higher, well-timed contact = faster serve

low.name: Contact too low / not reaching up (serve, overhead)
low.looks_like: Letting the serve toss drop before contact; hitting the ball below full stretch.
causes: Toss too low or dropping the tossing arm early
No leg drive up to the ball
Reaching out instead of up at contact
low.fix: Reach up and contact at the highest comfortable point; drive up from the legs.
low.feel: Stretch tall and hit at the top of the reach.

high.name: Over-reaching the contact
high.looks_like: Straining upward past a balanced contact, often chasing a toss thrown too high or too far back.
causes: Jumping for a ball out of position
high.fix: Settle the contact to a height you can repeat on balance.
high.feel: Tall but balanced, not a maximum stretch every time.

wrist_finish_height.label: Follow-through / finish height
wrist_finish_height.biomechanics: The follow-through is the receipt for everything before it. A high finish (racket up and over the opposite shoulder) proves the player accelerated UP through the ball - the low-to-high path that produces topspin - instead of decelerating into it. A short, low finish usually means a flat, braking swing. Modern 'windshield-wiper' forehands may finish anywhere from waist to over-the-shoulder depending on contact height, but the swing must still travel low-to-high through the ball.

pro_benchmarks.value: low-to-high, finishing high (over the shoulder) or wrapping to the waist on a wiper finish

low.name: Short / low finish (deceleration)
low.looks_like: Racket stops near the contact zone; little upward wrap.
low.fix: Finish higher, over the opposite shoulder, and hold it for a beat.
low.feel: Swing all the way up; let the racket wrap high.

high.name: Forced / stiff finish
high.looks_like: Exaggerated, tense follow-through that throws off balance.
high.fix: Relax the finish and let the follow-through breathe.
high.feel: Smooth, not stiff, at the end.

toss_height.label: Serve toss height
toss_height.biomechanics: The toss peak must sit slightly above the contact point so the ball drops into a perfectly timed contact. Too low rushes the swing and forces a downward hit into the net; too high adds a falling-ball timing error. The toss should be released with a straight tossing arm and placed slightly in front and to the racket side, not over the head (12 o'clock), to avoid impingement and downward swing paths.

pro_benchmarks.value: slightly lateral and in front, contact at ~100 deg arm abduction; toss peak just above contact
consistent toss > raw toss height

low.name: Toss too low
low.looks_like: Rushed swing, contact below full stretch, serves into the net.
low.fix: Toss higher with a straight arm and a smooth lift; reach up to the ball.
low.feel: Lift and release like placing the ball on a high shelf.

high.name: Toss too high
high.looks_like: Waiting on a falling ball; timing varies serve to serve.
causes: Throwing rather than lifting the toss
high.fix: Lower the toss so the peak is just above your contact point.
high.feel: The ball should barely 'hang' at the top before you hit it.

wrist_divergence.label: Toss-arm / racket-arm separation
wrist_divergence.biomechanics: On a sound serve the tossing arm and racket arm separate cleanly ('down together, up together' then split into the trophy): the tossing arm reaches up to release while the racket arm loads back and up. Poor separation - dropping the tossing arm early - collapses the shoulder tilt and drives the swing downward into the net.

pro_benchmarks.value: tossing arm extended up, racket arm cocked - both up, then separating

low.name: Tossing arm drops early / arms don't separate
low.looks_like: Tossing arm collapses as soon as the ball is released; shoulders level out.
causes: Pulling the tossing arm down too soon
low.fix: Keep the tossing arm up and pointed at the ball longer while the racket arm loads.
low.feel: Hold the toss-arm up like a statue until the racket drops.

high.name: Over-separated / out of sync
high.looks_like: Toss arm and racket arm wildly out of phase, breaking the trophy timing.
high.fix: Synchronise the toss with the trophy position - smooth and together.
high.feel: Both arms rise as one, then split on time.

prep_before_contact_ms.label: Preparation timing
prep_before_contact_ms.biomechanics: Early preparation buys time, and time is what separates clean contact from scrambling. Getting the unit turn and racket back before the ball bounces on your side lets the swing flow into a balanced contact; late prep cascades into late contact, a rushed swing and hurried footwork.

pro_benchmarks.value: ~600-1200 ms of preparation before contact

low.name: Late preparation
low.looks_like: Racket still going back as the ball arrives; everything is rushed.
causes: Slow reaction off the opponent's hit
Watching the ball instead of turning
low.fix: Racket back before the bounce on your side, every time. Say 'turn' as the ball crosses the net.
low.feel: Beat the bounce with your turn.

high.name: Rushing / over-early and frozen
high.looks_like: Prepared so early the swing stalls and tenses before contact.
causes: No rhythm between prep and swing
high.fix: Prepare early but let the swing breathe - smooth tempo, no hurry.

split_step_rate.label: Split-step rhythm
split_step_rate.biomechanics: The split step is a small hop timed to LAND just as the opponent strikes the ball. Landing loaded on the balls of the feet lets the player push off explosively in any direction, and primes reaction time. No split step = a flat-footed, late first move.

pro_benchmarks.value: land as the opponent contacts the ball, feet ~shoulder-width, knees soft

low.name: Missing / late split step
low.looks_like: Flat feet at the opponent's contact; slow, heavy first step.
low.fix: Hop a small split step on every opponent hit, landing on the balls of the feet.
low.feel: Land light and loaded exactly as they strike.

high.name: Too many / mistimed hops
high.looks_like: Bouncing constantly between balls, burning energy and mistiming the real split.
high.fix: Save the hop for the opponent's contact - one well-timed split, not constant jumping.
high.feel: Quiet feet, then one sharp split on their hit.

stance_width.label: Stance width (base)
stance_width.biomechanics: A base wider than the shoulders lowers the centre of mass, improves balance through contact and lets the player load the outside leg to drive into the shot. A narrow base tips over the moment the player swings hard or changes direction.

pro_benchmarks.value: feet at least shoulder-width, often wider on open-stance groundstrokes

low.name: Base too narrow
low.looks_like: Feet close together; off balance on hard or wide shots.
low.fix: Set the feet wider than the shoulders before the swing starts.
low.feel: Wide enough that a push couldn't topple you.

high.name: Base too wide / planted
high.looks_like: Feet so wide the player is stuck and can't adjust or recover.
high.fix: Bring the feet in a touch to a strong but mobile width.
high.feel: Wide and balanced, but still able to move.

recovery_steps.label: Recovery footwork
recovery_steps.biomechanics: Recovery decides whether a player hits ONE good shot or builds a whole point. After contact, quick shuffle or crossover steps back toward the middle (or the smart court position) re-centre the player before the next ball. Watching your own shot concedes the court.

pro_benchmarks.value: shuffle/crossover back toward center, low economical steps, re-split before the next ball

low.name: No recovery / admiring the shot
low.looks_like: Standing where the shot was hit; caught out of position on the reply.
low.fix: The shot isn't over at contact - two quick shuffles back toward the middle before the ball crosses the net.

high.name: Frantic / inefficient recovery
high.looks_like: Lots of small panicked steps, crossing the feet, never settling.
high.fix: Calm, controlled shuffle steps with rhythm - don't cross the feet.
high.feel: Smooth recovery, then a balanced split.

racquet_angle.label: Racquet angle at contact (racquet-arm line)
racquet_angle.biomechanics: The racquet tracker reads the frame as a rigid extension of the forearm (handle at the hand, frame tip ~1.35 torso lengths beyond it along the elbow->wrist line). Its angle from vertical at contact tells whether the racquet is swinging on the line of the shot with the head leading, or lagging and flailing behind the hand. A racquet arriving on-line keeps the strings square through the ball; a lagging or flicking head turns the face over unpredictably and sprays direction - on groundstrokes control leaks before power does. On the serve the same measure wants the racquet vertical (tip up) at a fully reached contact.
racquet_angle.sensing: Estimated from the forearm when no optical racquet detector is bundled, so it reads the racquet-ARM line, NOT the open/closed FACE twist (which needs a real racquet detector and is not sensible from a 2D body pose). View-gated to side/diagonal for groundstrokes and volleys; on the serve the tip-up measure is read from any view. When a racquet detector is added, the same metric is computed from the measured frame and handle keypoints with no downstream change.

pro_benchmarks.context: groundstroke contact - racquet extending on the line of the shot
pro_benchmarks.value: frame tip leading the hand, shaft roughly along the swing line

pro_benchmarks.context: serve contact - racquet vertical at full reach
pro_benchmarks.value: tip up, contact at ~100-110 deg arm abduction

low.name: Racquet head lagging / not extending through
low.looks_like: The racquet trails behind the hand at contact instead of swinging out on the line; contact feels wristy, late and behind the body.
causes: Flicking the wrist instead of swinging the head through
Late contact crowding the racquet behind the body
low.fix: Swing the racquet head out through the line of the shot toward your target - let the head lead, not drag behind the hand.
low.feel: Point the butt at the ball, then send the head out through to the target.

high.name: Racquet head flailing / face swinging open
high.looks_like: The head whips past the contact line and the face flings open, ballooning or spraying the shot.
causes: No control of the racquet head through contact
high.fix: Keep the racquet head controlled through the strike - punch the contact and quiet the wrist.
high.feel: Steady the face through the ball, finish with control.

racquet_height.label: Racquet reach at contact (serve / overhead)
racquet_height.biomechanics: The frame-tip height at contact on the serve and overhead is the racquet's reach. Striking with the racquet fully extended overhead maximises ball speed (the kinetic chain releases into a long lever) and the downward angle into the service box. Hitting below full stretch shortens the lever and flattens the trajectory into the net or long.
racquet_height.sensing: Frame-tip height estimated from the forearm extension; complements contact_height (the WRIST height) by adding the racquet length to the reach. Detector-upgradable like racquet_angle.

pro_benchmarks.value: contact near full stretch, ~100-110 deg arm abduction, racquet tip at the top of the reach

low.name: Striking below full reach
low.looks_like: Racquet tip not at the top of the reach at contact; the serve lacks pace and dips into the net.
causes: Toss too low or dropping the tossing arm early
No leg drive up to the ball
Reaching out instead of up at contact
low.fix: Stretch tall and meet the ball at the very top of the reach, tip to the sky, driving up from the legs.
low.feel: Hit at the top of a full stretch, not a comfortable one.

high.name: Overreaching the contact
high.looks_like: Straining past a balanced reach, usually chasing a toss thrown too high or behind.
causes: Jumping at a ball out of position
high.fix: Settle the contact to a height you can repeat on balance.
high.feel: Tall but balanced, not a maximum stretch every time.

racquet_drop.label: Racquet drop (serve loading)
racquet_drop.biomechanics: Behind the trophy the racquet head drops down the back (the 'back-scratch') before accelerating up to contact - the deeper, looser drop pre-stretches the shoulder and forearm for racquet-head speed. The tracker reports the frame-tip height at the racket-drop frame; a deep drop sits low behind the player.
racquet_drop.sensing: Tracked from the racquet estimate and surfaced for context, but NOT scored from pose alone - the back-scratch depth is foreshortened and unreliable in 2D, so it informs the coach without producing a deterministic cue. A racquet detector would make it scorable.

pro_benchmarks.context: serve racket drop (8-stage model, acceleration stage)
pro_benchmarks.value: deep, relaxed drop down the back before the drive up

low.name: Shallow racket drop
low.looks_like: Little or no back-scratch; the racquet starts up from a high, tight position.
causes: Rushing from the trophy to contact
low.fix: Let the racquet drop relaxed down the back before you drive up to the ball.
low.feel: Loose arm - let the head fall, then explode up.

high.name: Over-dropped / collapsed
high.looks_like: The arm collapses so far that the timing up to contact is lost.
high.fix: Keep the drop relaxed but connected to the leg drive and the toss.
high.feel: Drop and drive as one motion.

forehand.summary: Topspin drive built on an early unit turn, a low-to-high swing and contact out in front. Power comes from the legs and trunk (shoulder internal rotation supplies a large share of racket speed), not the arm.
forehand.typical_grip: Eastern or Semi-Western (Semi-Western is the most common modern forehand grip and the easiest route to topspin). Grip is NOT pose-measurable - coach it verbally.
key_checkpoints: Early shoulder turn before the bounce
Loaded legs / athletic knee bend
Slightly bent, relaxed elbow
Contact ~30-40 cm in front of the body
Racquet swinging on the line, head leading through contact
Low-to-high finish, balanced
forehand.racquet_position: Through contact the racquet should extend along the line of the shot with the frame tip leading the hand toward the target, not lagging behind a flicking wrist. The face angle (open/closed for slice/topspin) is grip- and wrist-driven and is NOT pose-measurable - coach it verbally.
common_faults: Not turning the body (arming the ball)
Wrong grip blocking topspin
Late preparation -> late contact
Racquet head lagging / flicking across the ball
Decelerating into a low finish

backhand.summary: One- or two-handed. The one-hander is an open kinetic chain needing a stable, fairly straight hitting arm and a contact point well in front; the two-hander is a closed chain - more stable, contact closer to the body, more reaction time.
backhand.typical_grip: One-hander: Eastern backhand / Continental hitting hand. Two-hander: dominant Continental + non-dominant Eastern/Semi-Western forehand. Grip is NOT pose-measurable.
key_checkpoints: Full shoulder coil (show the back) - more for the one-hander
Hitting arm set (straighter for the one-hander)
Contact in front: ~0.59 m one-handed, ~0.40 m two-handed
Drive up and through, balanced finish
backhand.racquet_position: Drive the racquet straight through the contact line; on the one-hander especially the head must lead out in front, not lag behind the body. Face twist is grip-driven and not pose-measurable.
common_faults: Bent/collapsing arm on the one-hander
Late or cramped contact
Racquet head lagging behind the hand
Turning hips and shoulders as one block (no coil)
backhand.notes: One- vs two-handed cannot be reliably told apart from pose alone; the engine scores the visible arm shape and contact geometry. The two-hand contact band is naturally a touch closer to the body.

serve.summary: The most complex stroke - an 8-stage chain (start, release, loading, cocking/trophy, acceleration, contact, deceleration, finish). The trophy is the fully loaded position: maximum knee flexion and lowest elbow, with the tossing arm up. Energy flows legs -> trunk -> shoulder internal rotation -> arm -> wrist into an upward, slightly-in-front contact.
serve.typical_grip: Continental (the universal serve grip; it enables the pronation/internal rotation that creates pace and spin). Using a forehand 'pancake' grip is a classic fault and is NOT pose-measurable.
key_checkpoints: Smooth straight-arm toss, slightly in front and to the racket side
Trophy: deep knee bend (~115 deg interior), elbow up, racket up, tossing arm up
Racket drop behind the back
Reach UP to a high contact (~100-110 deg arm abduction, elbow ~150 deg interior)
Drive the chest through; land inside the court
serve.racquet_position: At contact the racquet should be vertical (tip to the sky) at full reach; behind the trophy it drops down the back (the back-scratch) before driving up. The pronation that squares the face is grip- and forearm-driven and NOT pose-measurable.
common_faults: Toss too low / dropping the tossing arm early -> downward swing into the net
Pancake (forehand) grip preventing pronation
Shallow knee bend losing leg drive
Contact too low / racquet not reaching up to full stretch

volley.summary: A compact PUNCH at the net, not a swing. Short or no backswing, a firm wrist, contact clearly in front, and body weight stepped through the ball. Same Continental grip for both forehand and backhand volley so there's no time-consuming grip change.
volley.typical_grip: Continental for both wings. NOT pose-measurable.
key_checkpoints: Small unit turn (set the shoulders, no big takeback)
Low athletic base, knees bent
Firm wrist and arm - 'punch', don't swing
Contact clearly in front of the body
Short, held finish - step in with the weight
volley.racquet_position: A firm racquet face punched out in front - the head stays controlled, no droop or flick. Same Continental grip both wings; grip and face are not pose-measurable.
common_faults: Big backswing (slow reaction, less control)
Loose/whippy wrist (racquet head flailing)
Contact too late / beside the body
Standing tall instead of in a low base

footwork.summary: Scored over rolling ~10 s windows, not per shot. The movement skills that set up every stroke: a well-timed split step, a wide balanced base, and quick economical recovery. Low, light steps - not high-impact hops.
key_checkpoints: Split step landing as the opponent strikes
Base wider than the shoulders
Shuffle/crossover recovery back to position
Re-split before the next ball
common_faults: Flat-footed, no split step
Narrow base / off balance
Admiring the shot instead of recovering

principles.kinetic_chain: Power is generated proximal-to-distal: ground reaction -> legs -> hips -> trunk (X-factor) -> shoulder -> elbow -> wrist -> racket. Each link adds speed and any weak/late link leaks energy and raises injury risk. Most of the metrics above are checkpoints on this chain.
principles.ground_up: Bend the knees to load, drive up through contact. Stiff legs force the arm to do the work.
principles.contact_in_front: Across every stroke, contact in front of the body lets the player see the ball, transfer weight forward, and control direction.
principles.early_preparation: Turn and prepare before the bounce on your side; late prep cascades into late, rushed contact.
principles.balance_and_finish: If you can't hold a balanced finish for a beat, the swing wasn't balanced.
principles.consistency_over_power: Repeatable mechanics beat occasional big hits; small adjustments compound.
